ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Aug-10-2017 Continued To Sep-20-17 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jun-30-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Bennett Cohen, Esq. (415-864-3246) for Plaintiff.
Kristen Pico, Esq. (415-392-8300) of Hassard Bonnington LLP for Defendant Sutter Health and Sutter Bay.
Bret Landess, Esq. (415-392-8300) of Oium Reyen & Pryor for Defendant Margaret Chen, M.D.

RULING - ARGUED. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT MARGARET CHEN MD'S DEMURRER TO FOURTH CAUSE OF ACTION IN PLAINTIFF'S FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as to the fourth cause of action for bystander negligent infliction of emotional distress in the first amended complaint is SUSTAINED without leave to amend. Plaintiff James Karl has not alleged facts that show that he was contemporaneously aware that the defendants' actions during the labor and delivery of Baby Girl Karl caused Baby Girl Karl's death. Mr. Karl's alleges that the injury-causing events were the failure to diagnose fetal distress and the delay in delivery. The injuries resulting from those events are not amenable to a layperson's observations and thus they cannot satisfy the contemporaneous awareness requirement of a bystander NIED claim. (Bird v. Saenz (2002) 28 Cal.4th 910, 917-918.) While Mr. Karl has some medical experience and knowledge, Mr. Karl has not tied that experience and knowledge to his ability to detect injury causing events in the childbirth, particularly where the injury occurs in utero. Mr. Karl's presence during labor and delivery are insufficient to support a claim for bystander NIED. "[U]nderstanding perception of the injury-causing event is an essential component" of this claim. (Golstein v. Superior Court (1990) 223 Cal.App.3d 1415, 1427.) Because Mr. Karl has not alleged facts to show that he contemporaneously perceived and understood that the defendants' actions caused Baby Girl Karl's death and it does not appear reasonably possibly that he can do so in good faith, the demurrer to the fourth cause of action is sustained without leave to amend.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes) Order is signed. Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR 711, ckaren@sftc.org =(302/HK)

Plaintiff James Karl has not alleged facts that show that he was contemporaneously aware that the defendants' actions during the labor and delivery of Baby Girl Karl caused Baby Girl Karl's death. The injuries resulting from those events are not amenable to a layperson's observations and thus they cannot satisfy the contemporaneous awareness requirement of a bystander NIED claim. (Bird v. Saenz (2002) 28 Cal.4th 910, 917-918.) ORDER TO SHOW CAUSE
Off Calendar Feb-16-2017 Continued To May-09-17 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Feb-14-2017 Sustained
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Kristen A. Pico of the law offices of Hassard Bonnington LLP (415.288.9800) appears as counsel for defendants/demurring parties, Sutter Health and Sutter Bay Hospitals

No other appearance is made in this matter

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANTS SUTTER HEALTH and SUTTER BAY HOSPITALS DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT, as to the fourth cause of action for bystander negligent infliction of emotional distress alleged by plaintiff James Karl

SUSTAINED with twenty days leave to amend. If he can do so in good faith, Mr. Karl is given leave to allege facts showing that he was contemporaneously aware of the injury-producing event. In Ochoa v. Superior Court (1985) 39 Cal.3d 159 a boy confined in a juvenile detention facility died of pneumonia after authorities ignored his obviously serious symptoms, which included vomiting, coughing up blood, and excruciating pain. Ochoa permitted the mother, who observed the neglect and recognized it as harming her son, to sue as a bystander for negligent infliction of emotional distress. The court explained that "when there is observation of the defendant's conduct and the child's injury and contemporaneous awareness the defendant's conduct or lack thereof is causing harm to the child, recovery is permitted." (Ochoa, 39 Cal.3d at 170.) The injury-producing event in Ochoa was the failure of custodial authorities to respond significantly to symptoms obviously requiring immediate medical attention. Keys v. Alta Bates Summit Medical Center (2015) 235 Cal.App.4th 484 allowed recovery where the defendants showed no sense of urgency in treating the decedent's breathing problems. Bird v. Saenz (2002) 28 Cal.4th 910 held that the bystander negligent infliction of emotional distress cause of action could not be based on the negligent transection of the victim's artery because the plaintiff did not observe the injury-producing event. Bird further ruled that the claim could not be based on the subsequent negligence in failing to diagnose and treat the damaged artery. Bird explained that the plaintiff could not meaningfully perceive the negligence, stating that "Even if plaintiffs believed, as they stated in their declarations, that their mother was bleeding to death, they had no reason to know that the care she was receiving to diagnose and correct the cause of the problem was inadequate. While they eventually became aware that one injury-producing event-the transected artery-had occurred, they had no basis for believing that another, subtler event was occurring in its wake." (Bird, 28 Cal.4th at 917.) Mr. Karl's allegations are more like Bird than Ochoa and Keys. Paragraphs 6 and 17 of the complaint show that defendants were responding to Baby Girl Karl and Mr. Karl was observing those events. Mr. Karl does not allege sufficient facts showing how he could be aware of the injury-producing events. "Except in the most obvious cases, a misdiagnosis is beyond the awareness of lay bystanders." (Bird, 28 Cal.4th at 917.) Unlike Keys and Ochoa, defendants were timely responding to the urgency. This is not a failure to provide assistance case. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Jose Rios Merida; Reporter: Not Reported. (D302)
